chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Keeping the Time with the Skies

flashcards.q: What are the phases of the Moon?
flashcards.a: The changing shapes of the illuminated portion of the Moon as seen from Earth, resulting from the changing angle between the Sun, Moon, and Earth.

flashcards.q: Define a full Moon.
flashcards.a: The lunar phase when the entire illuminated hemisphere of the Moon faces Earth, appearing as a complete bright circle.

flashcards.q: Define a new Moon.
flashcards.a: The lunar phase when only the non-illuminated hemisphere of the Moon faces Earth, making the Moon invisible from Earth.

flashcards.q: What is the waxing period?
flashcards.a: The period when the bright portion of the Moon increases nightly, lasting ~15 days from new Moon to full Moon (Shukla Paksha).

flashcards.q: What is the waning period?
flashcards.a: The period when the bright portion of the Moon decreases nightly, lasting ~15 days from full Moon to new Moon (Krishna Paksha).

flashcards.q: Define a crescent Moon phase.
flashcards.a: A lunar phase showing less than half of the illuminated portion visible, appearing as a thin curved shape near the new Moon.

flashcards.q: Define a gibbous Moon phase.
flashcards.a: A lunar phase showing more than half but less than the complete illuminated portion, appearing as a bulging shape near the full Moon.

flashcards.q: How long does one complete cycle of Moon phases take?
flashcards.a: Approximately 29.5 days (nearly one month), from one full Moon to the next full Moon.

flashcards.q: What causes Moon phases?
flashcards.a: The changing angle between the Earth, Moon, and Sun as the Moon orbits Earth, which changes how much of the illuminated hemisphere is visible.

flashcards.q: Why does the Moon appear to have different shapes?
flashcards.a: Only different portions of the Moon's illuminated (sunlit) side face Earth as the Moon orbits. The Moon itself does not change shape.

flashcards.q: What is the difference between a lunar eclipse and Moon phases?
flashcards.a: Lunar eclipse: Earth's shadow falls on Moon, causing temporary darkening (rare, full Moon day only). Moon phases: Regular shape changes due to positional differences (occur every night, not caused by shadow).

flashcards.q: Why does the Moon rise approximately 50 minutes later each day?
flashcards.a: While Earth completes one rotation in 24 hours, the Moon continues moving in its orbit. Earth must rotate an additional ~50 minutes for the Moon to appear at the same position again.

flashcards.q: When is a waxing Moon easiest to observe?
flashcards.a: At sunset, when the Moon is visible in the western evening sky, moving away from the Sun.

flashcards.q: When is a waning Moon easiest to observe?
flashcards.a: At sunrise, when the Moon is visible in the eastern morning sky, moving toward the Sun.

flashcards.q: Define the mean solar day.
flashcards.a: The average time (24 hours) for the Sun to go from its highest position in the sky one day to its highest position the next day.

flashcards.q: How can the mean solar day be measured?
flashcards.a: By observing the length of shadows cast by an object during the day. The shadow is shortest when the Sun is at its highest point in the sky (solar noon).

flashcards.q: What is a lunar calendar?
flashcards.a: A calendar based on the phases of the Moon, with months of ~29.5 days and a year of ~354 days (12 lunar months).

flashcards.q: What is a solar calendar?
flashcards.a: A calendar based on Earth's revolution around the Sun, with a year of ~365 days, synchronized to keep seasons aligned to the same months yearly.

flashcards.q: What is a luni-solar calendar?
flashcards.a: A calendar combining lunar months (~29.5 days) with periodic intercalary month additions to maintain synchronization with seasons and keep the calendar year close to 365 days.

flashcards.q: How does a lunar calendar year compare to a solar year?
flashcards.a: Lunar year: 12 lunar months × 29.5 days = 354 days. Solar year: ~365 days. Difference: Lunar year is ~11 days shorter.

flashcards.q: What is the main disadvantage of a purely lunar calendar?
flashcards.a: Seasons do not remain synchronized to the same lunar months in successive years; lunar calendar loses seasonal alignment, shifting 11 days earlier each year.

flashcards.q: What is the main advantage of a solar calendar?
flashcards.a: Seasons remain synchronized to the same months yearly; spring always occurs in March-May, summer in June-August, etc., making agricultural planning predictable.

flashcards.q: What is a leap year?
flashcards.a: A year in the Gregorian calendar with an extra day (February 29), containing 366 days instead of 365, added to account for Earth's 365.25-day orbit.

flashcards.q: Why are leap years necessary?
flashcards.a: Earth takes 365.25 days to orbit the Sun. A 365-day calendar loses 0.25 day per year, accumulating to 1 full day every 4 years. Leap years add a day every 4 years to maintain alignment.

flashcards.q: What is an intercalary month (Adhika Maasa)?
flashcards.a: An extra month added periodically to luni-solar calendars to correct the accumulated deficit between the lunar year (354 days) and solar year (365 days).

flashcards.q: How often are intercalary months added in luni-solar calendars?
flashcards.a: Approximately every 2-3 years, when the accumulated 11-day annual deficit reaches ~29.5 days (one lunar month).

flashcards.q: What is the tropical year?
flashcards.a: The time period for Earth to complete one orbit and return to the same seasonal position relative to the Sun (spring equinox to spring equinox), approximately 365.2422 days.

flashcards.q: What is the sidereal year?
flashcards.a: The time period for Earth to return to the same position relative to the stars, approximately 365.2564 days; about 20 minutes longer than the tropical year.

flashcards.q: On which date does the Indian National Calendar begin?
flashcards.a: March 22 (or March 21 in leap years), the day after the spring equinox.

flashcards.q: Why was March 22 chosen as the start date for the Indian National Calendar?
flashcards.a: It is the day after the spring equinox (vernal equinox), an astronomically defined moment that marks the start of spring and ensures the calendar remains synchronized with seasons.

flashcards.q: Name three festivals tied to lunar phases.
flashcards.a: Diwali (new Moon of Kartika), Holi (full Moon of Phalguna), Buddha Purnima (full Moon of Vaisakha).

flashcards.q: Name three festivals based on solar cycles.
flashcards.a: Makar Sankranti, Pongal, Bihu (all occur around January 13-14).

flashcards.q: Why do festivals based on luni-solar calendars shift dates in the Gregorian calendar?
flashcards.a: Luni-solar calendars use lunar months that don't align with Gregorian months. Festival dates (defined by lunar phases) occur on different Gregorian dates yearly.

flashcards.q: What is the Rashtriya Panchang?
flashcards.a: An annual astronomical almanac published by India's Positional Astronomy Center, providing unified lunar phase dates and forecasting festival dates for the entire nation.

flashcards.q: Why does the Positional Astronomy Center publish the Rashtriya Panchang annually?
flashcards.a: To maintain nationwide uniformity in festival date declarations, accounting for regional sunrise time variations across India.

flashcards.q: What is an artificial satellite?
flashcards.a: A human-made object placed in orbit around Earth to perform functions like communication, navigation, weather monitoring, disaster management, or scientific research.

flashcards.q: What is the typical altitude of artificial satellites?
flashcards.a: Most satellites orbit about 800 km above Earth's surface, with orbital periods of approximately 100 minutes.

flashcards.q: Name five applications of artificial satellites.
flashcards.a: Communication, navigation (GPS), weather monitoring, disaster management, scientific research, Earth observation.

flashcards.q: What is the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO)?
flashcards.a: India's national space agency responsible for launching and managing satellites for communication, Earth observation, navigation, and scientific missions.

flashcards.q: Name three Cartosat satellite functions.
flashcards.a: Capture high-resolution Earth images for mapping, urban planning, disaster management assessment.

flashcards.q: What is the Bhuvan platform?
flashcards.a: An ISRO mapping platform using Cartosat satellite images to display terrain, soil composition, land use, vegetation, and geographic information.

flashcards.q: What is AstroSat?
flashcards.a: India's first dedicated space observatory (satellite telescope) that observes stars and celestial objects for scientific research.

flashcards.q: Name three Chandrayaan missions.
flashcards.a: Chandrayaan 1, Chandrayaan 2, Chandrayaan 3—lunar exploration missions studying the Moon's surface and searching for water.

flashcards.q: What is the Aditya L1 mission?
flashcards.a: ISRO's solar mission designed to study the Sun's corona and solar activities.

flashcards.q: What is the Mangalyaan mission?
flashcards.a: ISRO's Mars orbiter mission, demonstrating India's capability for interplanetary space missions.

flashcards.q: What is space debris (space junk)?
flashcards.a: Non-functional satellites, spent rocket stages, and collision fragments accumulating in orbit around Earth, posing hazard to working satellites.

flashcards.q: Why is space debris a concern?
flashcards.a: High-speed debris can collide with working satellites, damaging or destroying them and creating more debris in a cascading effect (Kessler syndrome).

flashcards.q: What is a geostationary satellite?
flashcards.a: A satellite with a 24-hour orbital period that remains stationary above one location on Earth's equator.

flashcards.q: At what altitude do geostationary satellites orbit?
flashcards.a: Approximately 35,786 kilometers above Earth's surface.

flashcards.q: What are the advantages of geostationary satellites?
flashcards.a: Remain fixed above one location, enabling permanent communication links without antenna adjustment; constant weather monitoring of one region.

flashcards.q: What is the disadvantage of geostationary satellites regarding signal delay?
flashcards.a: Signal delay of approximately 250 milliseconds due to the high altitude, unsuitable for real-time communication like video calls.

flashcards.q: How many geostationary satellites can theoretically orbit Earth?
flashcards.a: Approximately 120 satellites maximum (360° ÷ 3° minimum spacing) to avoid interference.

flashcards.q: What is the orbital period of satellites at 400 km altitude?
flashcards.a: Approximately 90 minutes, completing one orbit around Earth.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is the changing shape of the illuminated portion of the Moon.
flashcards.a: phase

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: The _____ period is when the bright portion of the Moon increases nightly.
flashcards.a: waxing

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: The _____ period is when the bright portion of the Moon decreases nightly.
flashcards.a: waning

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A lunar calendar year is approximately _____ days.
Fill in the blank: A solar year is approximately _____ days.
Fill in the blank: An _____ month is an extra month added to luni-solar calendars periodically.
flashcards.a: intercalary (or Adhika Maasa)

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: The Indian National Calendar begins on _____ (date).
flashcards.a: March 22 (or March 21 in leap years)

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: _____ is the annual astronomical almanac providing unified festival dates for India.
flashcards.a: Rashtriya Panchang

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: ISRO stands for Indian _____ Research Organisation.
flashcards.a: Space

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ satellite remains stationary above one location on Earth's equator.
flashcards.a: geostationary

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: _____ is non-functional satellites and rocket debris accumulating in orbit.
flashcards.a: Space debris (or Space junk)

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: The time for the Sun to reach its highest position one day to the next is called the _____ day.
flashcards.a: mean solar

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is a lunar phase showing less than half the illuminated portion.
flashcards.a: crescent

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ is a lunar phase showing more than half the illuminated portion.
flashcards.a: gibbous

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: The _____ equinox marks the start of spring (around March 20-21).
flashcards.a: spring (or vernal)

flashcards.q: True or False: Moon phases are caused by Earth's shadow falling on the Moon.
flashcards.a: False; phases are caused by changing positions of Earth, Moon, and Sun.

flashcards.q: True or False: The Moon appears to change shape because its physical shape changes nightly.
flashcards.a: False; only the visible illuminated portion changes; the Moon's physical shape remains spherical.

flashcards.q: True or False: Lunar eclipses occur during the waning period.
flashcards.a: False; lunar eclipses occur only on full Moon days when Earth is between the Sun and Moon.

flashcards.q: True or False: A lunar calendar year (354 days) is shorter than a solar year (365 days).
flashcards.a: True; the difference is approximately 11 days.

flashcards.q: True or False: Seasons remain synchronized to the same months in a purely lunar calendar.
flashcards.a: False; seasons drift through lunar months due to the 11-day annual deficit.

flashcards.q: True or False: A solar calendar requires leap years to maintain seasonal alignment.
flashcards.a: True; leap years account for Earth's 365.25-day orbit, preventing seasonal drift.

flashcards.q: True or False: Luni-solar calendars add intercalary months to correct seasonal drift.
flashcards.a: True; intercalary months every 2-3 years maintain synchronization.

flashcards.q: True or False: The Indian National Calendar begins on January 1.
flashcards.a: False; it begins on March 22 (day after the spring equinox).

flashcards.q: True or False: Diwali occurs on the same Gregorian date every year.
flashcards.a: False; Diwali's Gregorian date shifts yearly because it's based on lunar phases.

flashcards.q: True or False: Artificial satellites orbit Earth due to balance between gravity and orbital velocity.
flashcards.a: True; gravitational pull provides centripetal force for circular orbit.

flashcards.q: True or False: Geostationary satellites must orbit above the equator.
flashcards.a: True; geostationary orbit is restricted to the equatorial region (0° latitude).

flashcards.q: True or False: All artificial satellites have 24-hour orbital periods.
flashcards.a: False; most satellites have 90-100 minute periods; only geostationary satellites have 24-hour periods.

flashcards.q: True or False: Space debris is a significant collision hazard in low-Earth orbit.
flashcards.a: True; high satellite density and high orbital velocities create significant collision risk.

flashcards.q: True or False: The Moon rises at the same time each day.
flashcards.a: False; the Moon rises approximately 50 minutes later each day.

flashcards.q: True or False: The tropical year and sidereal year are exactly the same duration.
flashcards.a: False; the sidereal year is approximately 20 minutes longer than the tropical year.

flashcards.q: True or False: Kepler's third law relates orbital period to orbital altitude.
flashcards.a: True; orbital period² is proportional to orbital altitude³.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between orbital altitude and orbital period?
flashcards.a: As altitude increases, orbital period increases. Lower orbits have shorter periods; higher orbits have longer periods (Kepler's third law).

flashcards.q: Why can the Moon sometimes be visible during daytime?
flashcards.a: During the waxing period, the Moon is far from the Sun in the sky and has substantial bright portion, making it visible in the daylight sky when above the horizon.

flashcards.q: How does the 11-day annual deficit affect a lunar calendar over 33 years?
flashcards.a: The accumulated deficit reaches approximately 363 days (11 × 33), causing complete seasonal shift; festivals cycle through all seasons in 33 years.

flashcards.q: What astronomical event defines the start of the tropical year?
flashcards.a: The spring equinox (vernal equinox), when the Sun crosses the celestial equator moving northward.

flashcards.q: Why is the Gregorian calendar called a 'solar' calendar?
flashcards.a: It is based on Earth's revolution around the Sun (tropical year), keeping seasons synchronized to the same months yearly.

flashcards.q: How do luni-solar calendars maintain both lunar month identity and seasonal accuracy?
flashcards.a: By keeping lunar months (~29.5 days) for monthly counting but adding intercalary months every 2-3 years to correct the seasonal drift and maintain synchronization.

flashcards.q: What would happen to the orbital period if a satellite were moved to a higher altitude?
flashcards.a: The orbital period would increase; a satellite at higher altitude orbits more slowly and takes longer to complete one orbit.

flashcards.q: Explain the significance of the 29.5-day lunar month in calendar design.
flashcards.a: The 29.5-day lunar cycle is the natural basis for lunar months; 12 lunar months (~354 days) defines a lunar year, though it's 11 days shorter than the solar year.

flashcards.q: What is the primary difference between a month in a solar calendar and a lunar month?
flashcards.a: A solar calendar month is a fixed division of the 365-day year (~30.4 days); a lunar month is the duration of the Moon's phase cycle (~29.5 days).
